copyright: Logan Creek Suspension Bridge, West Coast Trail, Canada (© Tandem Stock/Adobe Stock)
description: Running along the remote southwest coast of Vancouver Island in Canada, the West Coast Trail is one of the country's most legendary hikes. Located in British Columbia's Pacific Rim National Park Reserve, the trail stretches about 47 miles through rainforest, beaches, and rocky shoreline. It was established in 1907 as a lifesaving route for shipwreck survivors along what sailors once called the 'Graveyard of the Pacific.'

copyright: Juvenile manatees in a freshwater spring, Crystal River, Florida (© Gregory Sweeney/Getty Images)
description: Manatees may seem slow and sleepy, but they have some surprising tricks under the surface. For starters, they are one of the few mammals that constantly replace their teeth. As old molars wear down from chewing gritty seagrass, new ones slowly roll forward like a conveyor belt—handy for an animal that eats for up to eight hours a day.

copyright: Cherry blossoms at East Lake Cherry Blossom Park, Wuhan, China (© Zhang Qiao/VCG/Getty Images)
description: From above, East Lake Cherry Blossom Park in Wuhan, China, looks like a soft lilac cloud settled gently over the land. Thousands of cherry trees bloom at once, their pale petals forming a living canopy around a golden five-story pagoda at the park's heart. The pagoda rises in calm symmetry, its tiered rooflines glowing against the delicate pastels of spring.

copyright: Lightning storm over saguaro cacti, Sonoran Desert, Arizona (© Jack Dykinga/Nature Picture Library)
description: The strength of nature is nowhere more evident than in powerful storms. Scary? Yes. Fascinating? Even more so. Learning to understand and respect these forces helps humanity live more safely.

Today is World Water Day, declared by the UN in 1992 and first observed the following year. Humanity could no longer drift along—we had to safeguard the very currents that keep us alive. Few places capture that urgency like Lake Tanganyika, in the Great Rift Valley of eastern Africa. From space, it's a roughly 420‑mile ribbon linking Tanzania, Burundi, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Zambia. At the shoreline, it's a 10-million-year-old rift basin, formed as the Earth's crust slowly pulled apart.

copyright: Letea Forest, Danube Delta, Romania (© Wild Wonders of Europe/Widstrand/Nature Picture Library)
description: If Earth had a maintenance crew, forests would be spearheading it. They absorb carbon emissions, reduce soil erosion, safeguard biodiversity, influence rainfall, and help maintain clean water supplies. They still cover about 31% of the world's land area, yet their continued decline shows how vulnerable these natural systems have become. Vast stretches continue to be cleared for agriculture, infrastructure, and resource extraction, weakening ecosystems at an alarming rate.

A white snowdrop is spring's quiet debut. No confetti, no countdown—just a delicate flower appearing as the spring equinox balances day and night. Snowdrops are among the first to bloom, often pushing through cold soil before winter fully packs up. Their drooping, bell-shaped flowers may look delicate, but they're built for early arrival. Special antifreeze proteins in their cells help them tolerate frost, making them well suited to unpredictable late-winter weather.

copyright: Short-beaked echidna, Adelaide Hills, Australia (© Etienne Littlefair/naturepl.com)
